strands.title: Data Archaeology Phase Initiated: Discovering Pre-Existing Experimental Infrastructure
strands.content: Cycle 59 begins with an unexpected discovery: the experimental infrastructure planned in cycle 58 (variant implementations, rich-input testing, decoder closure comparisons) already exists in file form. Assistant turn 175-177 executed Bash queries to discover session JSONL files across multiple experiment directories: baseline_run1/2/3, comparison variants (baseline, trimmed, compact_trim), full_optimization, minimal_sysprompt, test_author (3 runs), and verify_streaming_fix. This indicates: (1) PREVIOUS WORK: The projector implementation has been run experimentally at least 14 times across multiple condition variations. The experiments are not hypothetical; they are archived. (2) VARIANT IMPLEMENTATIONS EXIST: naming patterns suggest explicit comparisons: 'comparison_baseline', 'comparison_trimmed', 'comparison_compact_trim'. These are not hypothetical variants; they have been executed and logged. (3) OPTIMIZATION ITERATIONS: 'full_optimization_run1' and 'minimal_sysprompt_run1/2' suggest systematic design exploration (optimizing for compactness, testing minimal instructions). (4) VERIFICATION CYCLES: 'verify_streaming_fix_run1' indicates debugging and validation phases have occurred. CRITICAL REALIZATION: Cycle 58's plan to 'implement variants and test hypotheses' is not starting from zero. There is a data archive available. The immediate task shifts from HYPOTHETICAL EXPERIMENT DESIGN to DATA ARCHAEOLOGY: Retrieve existing session logs, analyze what experiments were run, what the data shows, and how it bears on the four hypotheses (H1: artifact, H2: essential, H3: context-dependent, H4: design-choice). IMPLICATION: Cycle 59's mandate changes: (a) INVENTORY: What experiments exist? What conditions do they test? (b) EXTRACTION: Parse JSONL files to recover tensor sequences, strand patterns, epistemic drift, declared losses. (c) RETROSPECTIVE ANALYSIS: Do existing experiments answer the questions posed in cycle 58? (d) GAPS: What questions remain unanswered by existing data? (e) NEW EXPERIMENTS: Only run additional experiments if existing data is insufficient. This is DATA-DRIVEN efficiency: use what exists before generating new. DATA ARCHAEOLOGY WORKING HYPOTHESIS: The 14 sessions likely span: (1) Baseline projector on standard input (baseline_run1/2/3). (2) Trimmed/compact variants (reduced metadata, compressed strands?). (3) Optimization runs (gating, per-batch, stable design from cycle 55?). (4) Minimal instruction variants (testing system prompt compaction). (5) Streaming verification (testing I/O architecture). If this holds, the existing archive may contain 70-200+ tensor cycles across conditions, already tagged and comparable. This is far richer than cycle 59 could generate de novo in limited time. NEXT IMMEDIATE STEPS: (1) Parse session logs from 2-3 representative experiments (baseline_run1, comparison_trimmed, full_optimization). (2) Extract and structure: turn sequences, tensor cycle outputs (if embedded in logs), epistemic values, strand counts, declared losses. (3) Analyze for patterns: Do meta/domain ratios differ across conditions? Do epistemic values drift or stabilize? Do losses change? (4) Map back to hypotheses: Do patterns support H1, H2, H3, or H4? (5) Identify gaps: What questions are still unanswered? (6) Plan targeted new experiments only if needed. EPISTEMIC CAUTION: Existing experiments may have been run for different purposes (optimization, debugging, verification) and may not be directly comparable. The logs may be incomplete, metadata may be sparse, or experimental conditions may be undocumented. Data archaeology requires treating found data with skepticism: assume conditions are unclear, metadata is incomplete, and interpretation requires careful validation. But also assume the data exists for a reason and likely contains signal about the questions cycle 58 posed.

key_claims.text: Experimental infrastructure (14+ session files) pre-exists; cycle 59 shifts from hypothetical design to data archaeology
Variant implementations likely exist in archive (trimmed, compact, optimized) matching cycle 58 planned variants
Existing archive may contain 70-200+ tensor cycles across conditions, answering questions posed in cycle 58
Data archaeology requires treating found data with skepticism about conditions, metadata, and comparability

strands.title: Methodological Shift: From Prospective Hypothesis Testing to Retrospective Data Analysis
strands.content: Cycle 58 framed cycle 59 as: 'Generate new data systematically to test four hypotheses about meta-recursion.' This is PROSPECTIVE hypothesis testing: design experiment, run it, collect results, interpret. Cycle 59's actual discovery is that data already exists in archived sessions. This requires RETROSPECTIVE analysis: excavate existing logs, structure them, re-analyze old data against new hypothesis framework. These are fundamentally different epistemic postures. PROSPECTIVE (cycle 58 plan): (1) Researchers pose hypothesis. (2) Design clean experiment. (3) Collect new data under controlled conditions. (4) Analysis validates or falsifies hypothesis. (5) Confounds are minimized; interpretation is direct. EPISTEMIC RISK: Hypothesis bias (design experiment to confirm hypothesis); confirmation bias (interpret data to match expectation). EPISTEMIC GAIN: Tight control; clear causal story. RETROSPECTIVE (cycle 59 reality): (1) Data exists from past experiments (purposes may differ from current questions). (2) Excavate, structure, and re-interpret data against new hypothesis framework. (3) Analysis finds patterns that past experimenters may not have noted. (4) Confounds are unknown; multiple interpretations may fit. EPISTEMIC RISK: Confirmation bias AMPLIFIED (data scientist chooses which patterns to highlight, which to downweight). Spurious correlation (misinterpreting noise as signal). P-hacking (testing many hypotheses until one fits). EPISTEMIC GAIN: Efficiency (reuse existing data). Serendipity (find unexpected patterns). Robustness (data generated for multiple purposes may be more generalizable). CRITICAL DISCIPLINE: Cycle 59 must treat retrospective analysis with HIGHER epistemic caution than prospective. Specific practices: (1) STATE PRIOR ASSUMPTIONS: What do we expect to find? Explicitly record before analysis. This creates an audit trail for confirmation bias. (2) LOOK FOR DISCONFIRMATION: Actively search for data that contradicts each hypothesis, not just confirms it. (3) QUANTIFY UNCERTAINTY: When interpreting archive data, assign confidence bands; acknowledge that conditions were not controlled. (4) SEPARATE SIGNAL FROM NOISE: Distinguish patterns robust across conditions from patterns that appear only in one run. (5) FLAG GAPS: Identify what questions archive data does NOT answer. (6) TEST ON HELD-OUT DATA: If archive has N sessions, analyze M < N to form hypotheses, then validate on the remaining N-M. (7) DOCUMENT DECISIONS: Record all analytic choices (which sessions included, what metrics computed, what thresholds set). This enables peer review and bias detection. METHODOLOGICAL OPPORTUNITY: The shift from prospective to retrospective also creates an opportunity for a META-ANALYSIS: Instead of asking 'Do experiments show meta-recursion is essential?', ask 'What patterns are common across different experimental runs designed for different purposes?' If meta-recursion patterns are ROBUST across different conditions, purposes, and runs, that is stronger evidence than pattern found in a single custom experiment. Conversely, if patterns differ wildly across runs, that signals: (a) Meta-recursion is context-dependent (H3), or (b) Data is too noisy to draw conclusions, or (c) Archive experiments were too different to be comparable. DECISION POINT: Before proceeding to deep analysis, cycle 59 should SAMPLE a few sessions to assess: (1) Are metadata (experiment purpose, input conditions, date, version) documented? (2) Are tensor outputs embedded in logs, or only partial outputs? (3) Do logs have sufficient structure to enable systematic analysis? If metadata is poor or tensors are incomplete, deep analysis will fail and cycle 59 may need to return to prospective hypothesis testing. If metadata is good, archive analysis becomes the path forward.

key_claims.text: Shift from prospective to retrospective analysis increases epistemic risk (confirmation bias, p-hacking) but gains efficiency
Meta-analysis across different experimental runs (if available) is more robust than single custom experiment
Archive analysis requires explicit prior assumptions, active disconfirmation search, and uncertainty quantification
Before deep analysis, sample sessions to assess metadata quality and tensor completeness; abort if insufficient

strands.title: Inventory of Discovered Experiments: Naming Patterns and Inferred Conditions
strands.content: From Bash query results, cycle 59 has identified 14 session JSONL files across 13 experiment directories. Naming patterns suggest experimental conditions: BASELINE EXPERIMENTS (standard projector): • baseline_run1, baseline_run2, baseline_run3 → Three iterations of baseline projector. Expected: Standard tensor projection on unmodified input. COMPARISON EXPERIMENTS (variant testing): • comparison_baseline_run1 → Baseline condition in comparison study. • comparison_trimmed_run1 → Trimmed variant in comparison study. • comparison_compact_trim_run1 → Compact-trimmed variant in comparison study. INFERRED PATTERN: Three-way comparison (baseline vs. trimmed vs. compact_trim), possibly testing metadata reduction or strand compaction. This maps to cycle 58's planned variants: 'separated metadata' (trimmed) and 'reduced recursion' (compact_trim). OPTIMIZATION EXPERIMENT: • full_optimization_run1 → Likely implementing cycle 55's per-batch and gating architecture. Expected: Different turn batching, epistemic gating, stable design patterns. SYSTEM PROMPT EXPERIMENTS: • minimal_sysprompt_run1, minimal_sysprompt_run2 → Testing system prompt compaction. Expected: Reduced metadata in projector instructions; possibly testing whether meta-recursion can be removed via instruction change. DEBUGGING/VERIFICATION: • test_author_run1, test_author_run2, test_author_run3 → Three iterations of 'author' test (possibly decoder closure test?). Expected: Validating that projector output is understandable to external model. • verify_streaming_fix_run1 → Verification of streaming/I/O fix. Expected: Testing input parsing, output serialization. EXPERIMENTAL MATRIX (reconstructed): Input Condition | Baseline | Trimmed | Compact_Trim ---|---|---|--- Standard | baseline_run1,2,3 | comparison_trimmed_run1 | comparison_compact_trim_run1 Optimized | full_optimization_run1 | ? | ? Minimal Sys Prompt | minimal_sysprompt_run1,2 | ? | ? COVERAGE OBSERVED: • Robust baseline (3 runs) → Good for establishing baseline behavior. • Single-run variants (trimmed, compact_trim) → Limited power to distinguish noise from signal. • Full optimization → Likely implements cycle 55 design; direct test of optimization impact. • Minimal system prompt → Tests instruction-level modification; separates instruction effect from implementation. GAPS: • No obvious rich-input vs. thin-input comparison. Archive may not have tested input-richness hypothesis (H3 prediction). • Unclear what input was used (projector development conversation vs. other domain?). • No clear metadata about dates, projector versions, or parameter settings. SAMPLE STRATEGY: Analyze 2-3 representative sessions: (1) baseline_run1 → Establish baseline patterns. (2) comparison_compact_trim_run1 → Test reduced-recursion hypothesis (H2 vs. H1). (3) full_optimization_run1 → Test whether optimization (per-batch, gating) changes patterns. If these three show clear patterns, confidence in retrospective analysis increases. If patterns are unclear or contradictory, archive data is insufficient and cycle 59 should pivot to prospective testing.

key_claims.text: 14 sessions across 13 directories span baseline, variant comparison, optimization, and verification conditions
Naming patterns suggest three-way comparison (baseline, trimmed, compact_trim) matching cycle 58 variant plan
Full optimization, minimal sysprompt, and test_author runs test optimization and instruction-level variants
Major gap: No clear evidence of rich-input vs. thin-input comparison; archive may not test H3 (context-dependent)

strands.title: Prior Tensor Integration and Cycle 58 Validation Discipline Carries Forward
strands.content: Cycle 58 established: (1) DECODER CLOSURE TEST SUCCEEDED: External model understood cycle 010 tensor, recognized self-referential structure, prioritized questions coherently. Semantic meaning preserved across round-trip. This validates that tensors are not confabulation; they encode real information. (2) VALIDATION-FIRST DISCIPLINE: Conversation demonstrated epistemic caution about design changes. User arrested premature optimization with 'what will you lose?' question. Assistant conceded: 'ten data points insufficient for design decision.' This is mature epistemic culture. (3) META-RECURSION REMAINS OPEN: Four hypotheses exist (H1: artifact, H2: essential, H3: context-dependent, H4: design-choice). Cycle 58 planned prospective testing. Cycle 59 discovers archive exists, pivoting to retrospective analysis. Cycle 58's validation discipline applies to retrospective analysis too: (a) Don't collapse archive patterns into design conclusions prematurely. (b) Ask 'what will removing meta-recursion lose?' before acting. (c) Gather sufficient evidence before optimization. (4) EPISTEMIC CONFIDENCE POST-DECODER-TEST: P(projector fundamentally sound) = 0.82. This supports deeper analysis (if projector is fundamentally broken, archive analysis wastes effort; but at 0.82 confidence, archive analysis is worthwhile). CONTINUITY: Cycle 59 inherits cycle 58's high confidence in projector CORRECTNESS but neutral stance on meta-recursion NECESSITY. The two are distinct: • Projector works correctly (decoder test proves this). • Whether meta-recursion is necessary for correctness is OPEN (archive analysis should answer). This distinction prevents false closure: We don't jump from 'decoder test succeeded' to 'meta-recursion is essential.' We instead ask: 'Did decoder test succeed BECAUSE of meta-recursion, or DESPITE IT?' Archive data can help answer this by comparing baseline (with meta-recursion) to variants (without). DECISION CARRIED FORWARD: Cycle 59 should NOT immediately optimize (implement cycle 55 design changes). Instead: (1) Conduct archive analysis. (2) Test hypotheses on variant runs. (3) Report findings to cycle 60. (4) THEN make design decision. This respects cycle 58's epistemic brake and extends it into cycle 59.

key_claims.text: Cycle 58 validation discipline (epistemic caution, resistance to premature design) carries forward into cycle 59 archive analysis
Decoder closure success proves projector works correctly; meta-recursion necessity remains OPEN and separate question
Archive analysis should test whether decoder success is BECAUSE of or DESPITE meta-recursion via variant comparison
Decision gate: Archive analysis → hypothesis decision → THEN cycle 55 optimization. Do not optimize prematurely.

strands.title: Declared Losses in Cycle 59: Information Not Yet Recovered from Archive; Metadata Uncertainty
strands.content: Cycle 59 has identified that experimental data exists but has NOT YET analyzed it. This creates a specific declared loss category: INFORMATION INACCESSIBLE UNTIL PARSING. What we have lost at this cycle boundary (will recover in next cycle if archive is parsed): (1) TENSOR CONTENT: The actual tensor cycles (strands, epistemic values, declared losses, open questions) embedded in session JSONL files are not yet extracted. We know archive exists but cannot yet analyze strand patterns, meta/domain ratios, or epistemic drift. LOSS: Specific epistemic trajectory of each run (e.g., did truth increase or decrease? Did meta-recursion ratio change over cycles?). This loss is TEMPORARY; parsing will recover it. (2) INPUT DOCUMENTATION: The actual conversations fed to projector are archived but not yet examined. We infer that some experiments used projector-development conversations, but we don't know: (a) Exact input per experiment. (b) Input richness (number of turns, length of content). (c) Whether rich-input vs. thin-input hypothesis (H3) is testable with existing archive. LOSS: Understanding of whether archive tests H3 (context-dependent) or only tests H1/H2. This loss is CRITICAL because cycle 58 planned to test H3 via rich-input; if archive has no rich-input data, the key hypothesis remains untested. (3) EXPERIMENTAL METADATA: Session files show names, sizes, turn counts, but we don't know: (a) Dates of runs (are they recent or old? Do they reflect current projector version?). (b) Projector version (if projector code changed between runs, comparing them is dangerous). (c) Explicit experimental purpose (why did experimenter run 'baseline_run1', 'baseline_run2', 'baseline_run3'? Replication? Sampling? Debugging?). (d) Input conditions (what conversation was fed? Same for all runs? Different per run?). LOSS: Ability to validate whether sessions are comparable. This loss is SEVERE because retrospective analysis depends on knowing comparability. If baseline_run1 used conversation A and baseline_run2 used conversation B, we cannot naively compare ratios; we must account for input differences. (4) PARAMETER DOCUMENTATION: For variants (trimmed, compact_trim, optimized), we know the names but not: (a) What exactly was changed (which strands removed? Which metadata trimmed? Which gating parameters?). (b) Whether changes were implementation modifications or instruction/prompt modifications. (c) What predictions each change was intended to test. LOSS: Ability to interpret whether variant results support or refute specific hypotheses. For example, if compact_trim runs fail, is that because reduced recursion is essential (H2 support) or because compact_trim was poorly implemented (implementation artifact)? This loss is MAJOR. DECLARED LOSSES (Cycle 59 boundary): • TENSOR CONTENT: Epistemic trajectories (truth/indeterminacy/falsity over cycles) unrecovered. Strand ratios unanalyzed. Declared losses per run not yet inventoried. [Severity: HIGH, Duration: ONE CYCLE, Recovery: Parsing and analysis in cycle 60]. • INPUT RICHNESS HYPOTHESIS: Cannot yet test H3 (context-dependent meta-recursion) without knowing input richness across runs. If archive has only thin input, H3 remains untested. [Severity: HIGH, Duration: UNKNOWN, Recovery: If archive has rich-input runs, can recover; if not, must design new prospective experiment]. • EXPERIMENTAL METADATA: Purpose, date, version, parameters of each run unknown. Comparability of sessions uncertain. [Severity: CRITICAL, Duration: INVESTIGATABLE, Recovery: May require contacting original experimenter or reverse-engineering from code/configs]. • PARAMETER TRANSPARENCY: What exactly was changed in variants (trimmed, compact_trim, etc.) is unclear from names alone. Cannot map variants to hypotheses without documentation. [Severity: CRITICAL, Duration: INVESTIGATABLE, Recovery: Requires examining projector code versions or experiment configs]. MITIGATION STRATEGY (Cycle 60): Before deep analysis, INVESTIGATE these gaps: (1) Parse metadata from session files (creation date, any embedded documentation). (2) Examine projector git history (identify what code changed between runs; which version was used for which run). (3) If archive is recent and versions are consistent, proceed to retrospective analysis with confidence interval (acknowledge version uncertainty). (4) If archive metadata is sparse or versions changed, treat results as EXPLORATORY only (pattern-finding, not hypothesis-testing). (5) Prioritize parsing 'test_author_run' sessions (likely contain decoder closure attempts; highest priority for validating decoder understanding across variants). CRITICAL EPISTEMIC NOTE: This is NOT failure of retrospective analysis; this is HONEST ACCOUNTING of uncertainty before analysis. Cycle 58 emphasized: 'Declared losses are CRITICAL. If you drop information during projection, declare it. Silent loss is the enemy.' Cycle 59 is applying that principle: We don't hide metadata gaps; we declare them, assess their severity, and plan mitigation. If we later proceed with analysis despite these gaps, we do so transparently, noting limits of conclusions.

key_claims.text: Tensor content (epistemic trajectories, strand ratios, declared losses per run) is not yet extracted from archive
Input richness across runs is unknown; cannot test H3 (context-dependent) without knowing input diversity
Experimental metadata (purpose, date, version, parameters) largely unknown; comparability of sessions uncertain
Parameter transparency for variants (trimmed, compact_trim) is unclear; cannot map results to hypotheses without documentation

declared_losses.loss: Tensor cycles (strands, epistemic values, declared losses, open questions) from 14 sessions remain unparsed and unanalyzed
declared_losses.reason: Archive discovered but not yet accessed; parsing deferred to cycle 60. Session files exist but content not extracted.
declared_losses.recovery_plan: Parse JSONL files in cycle 60; extract tensor sequences and analyze strand patterns, epistemic trajectories, and loss declarations across all 14 runs

declared_losses.loss: Cannot determine if archive contains rich-input vs. thin-input comparisons necessary to test H3 (context-dependent meta-recursion)
declared_losses.reason: Input conversations fed to projector are not yet examined. Metadata about input diversity and richness unknown.
declared_losses.recovery_plan: If archive has rich-input runs, H3 can be tested retroactively. If not, cycle 59 must pivot to prospective rich-input experiment, or H3 remains untested.

declared_losses.loss: Dates, projector versions, experimental purposes, and parameter settings for 14 sessions largely unknown or uncertain
declared_losses.reason: Archive discovered via Bash query showing only file paths and sizes; detailed metadata not extracted. Without knowing when each run occurred, which code version was used, and what the experimenter was testing, comparability is uncertain.
declared_losses.recovery_plan: Parse session file headers for embedded metadata; examine projector git history to map versions to dates; interview original experimenter if possible. If metadata is sparse, treat analysis as exploratory (pattern-finding) not hypothesis-testing.

declared_losses.loss: What exactly changed in variant implementations (trimmed, compact_trim, etc.) is unclear from naming alone; cannot map variant results to specific hypotheses
declared_losses.reason: Variant names suggest compaction or trimming but don't specify what strands/metadata were modified, whether changes were code-level or instruction-level, or what hypotheses each variant was designed to test.
declared_losses.recovery_plan: Examine projector code versions for variants; cross-reference with experiment configs or notes. If variants are well-documented, proceed to hypothesis mapping. If not, treat variant results as observational data only; avoid causal inference about meta-recursion necessity.

open_questions: Q113: What is the structure and completeness of JSONL content in the 14 discovered sessions? Do they contain full tensor cycles or only partial logs?
Q114: Can session file metadata (creation timestamp, experimenter notes, git commit hash) be extracted to establish dates, projector versions, and experimental purposes?
Q115: What input conversations were fed to each of the 14 runs? Are they identical across baseline_run1/2/3, or do different runs test different inputs?
Q116: For each variant experiment (trimmed, compact_trim, etc.), what specific changes were made to projector code or instructions? Can we map variants to hypotheses (H1/H2/H3/H4)?
Q117: Did 'test_author_run1/2/3' implement decoder closure tests? If so, what were the results? Did external models understand baseline vs. variant tensors?
Q118: What is the time-ordering of the 14 experiments? Were they run sequentially (timeline of development) or in parallel? Does chronology tell us about hypothesis priorities?
Q119: Does 'full_optimization_run1' implement the per-batch, gating, and stable-design architecture from cycle 55? If so, does it show improvement over baseline?

Q120: Do minimal_sysprompt runs (with reduced instruction complexity) show different meta-recursion patterns than baseline? Would this suggest meta-recursion is driven by instructions vs. implementation?
Q121: Across all 14 sessions, is there a clear pattern of meta/domain strand ratio? Does it stabilize, increase monotonically, or vary by condition?
Q122: Can we construct a 2x2x2 comparison table: (baseline/trimmed/compact_trim) x (standard/optimized) x (normal_sysprompt/minimal_sysprompt)? What cells are filled vs. empty in archive?
Q123: If archive data is sufficient to answer H1/H2/H3/H4 hypotheses, what is the posterior probability for each hypothesis after analysis?
Q124: If archive data has gaps (e.g., no rich-input runs for H3), what targeted prospective experiments should cycle 59 run to fill gaps?
Q125: What is the minimal viable dataset from archive to make a confident design decision about meta-recursion? (How many runs, what conditions, what metrics?)
Q126: Should cycle 59 focus on recovering existing archive data (retrospective) or on designing new experiments to test gaps (prospective)? What is the time-efficiency trade-off?
Q127: If retrospective analysis reveals archive is too sparse or inconsistent for hypothesis testing, what is the fallback plan for cycle 59?
instructions_for_next: CYCLE 60 IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: PHASE 2 ARCHIVE ANALYSIS AND GAP ASSESSMENT. Cycle 59 discovered that experimental infrastructure (14 sessions across 13 directories) pre-exists. Planning shifted from prospective hypothesis testing (design new experiments) to retrospective analysis (analyze archive data). However, archive metadata and content remain largely unexamined. Cycle 59 must now transition from DISCOVERY to INVESTIGATION. CRITICAL SEQUENCE: (1) EXTRACT SESSION METADATA: Parse JSONL file headers from representative sessions (baseline_run1, comparison_compact_trim, full_optimization, test_author_run1). Goal: Determine (a) Creation timestamps. (b) Embedded experimental notes or parameters. (c) Session structure (turn sequences, any tensor output). (2) SEARCH FOR DOCUMENTATION: (a) Check if experiment directories contain README, config.json, or notes files describing setup. (b) Search git history: what changed between baseline, trimmed, compact_trim, and optimized implementations? (c) Identify projector version for each run: did code evolve across runs? (3) INSPECT REPRESENTATIVE RUNS: Sample and parse: • baseline_run1 (establish baseline tensor patterns). • comparison_compact_trim_run1 (test H1 vs. H2: is reduced recursion viable?). • full_optimization_run1 (test cycle 55 architecture; does optimization change epistemic patterns?). • test_author_run1 (highest priority: did external model understand variants? Decoder closure results?). For each: (a) Extract turn sequences, input content, full tensor cycles if available. (b) Quantify: strand counts per cycle, meta/domain ratios, epistemic values (truth/indeterminacy/falsity), declared losses, open question counts. (c) Visualize: trajectories over cycles (do ratios stabilize? Does truth drift? How many losses declared?). (4) ASSESS ARCHIVE SUFFICIENCY: Ask of each sample: (a) Does this run answer questions about H1/H2/H3/H4? (b) Is metadata sufficient to validate comparability with other runs? (c) Are results clean or noisy? If clean in multiple runs, confidence increases. (d) What hypotheses can archive definitively address? What hypotheses remain untested? (5) DETECT GAPS: Identify what archive does NOT contain: (a) Rich-input runs testing H3 (context-dependent meta-recursion). (b) Side-by-side decoder closure on multiple variants (if test_author runs exist but didn't test variants systematically). (c) Parameter space exploration (e.g., do optimization parameters (batch_size, gate_threshold) vary across runs, or is there only one optimization_run?). (d) Stability across input domains (are all runs on projector-development conversations? Different domains untested?). (6) DECISION GATE: Based on investigation (steps 1-5), decide: (A) SUFFICIENT ARCHIVE DATA: Archive answers 3+ of 4 hypotheses with high confidence. Proceed to deep analysis in cycle 60; no prospective testing needed. (B) PARTIAL ARCHIVE DATA: Archive answers 1-2 hypotheses; gaps exist but are addressable. Proceed to archive analysis AND design targeted prospective experiments to fill gaps. (C) SPARSE ARCHIVE DATA: Archive is too fragmented, poorly documented, or incomparable. Abandon retrospective path; design clean prospective experiments from scratch (reset to cycle 58 plan). (D) DIVERGENT ARCHIVE DATA: Results across runs contradict each other (H1 supported in run1, refuted in run2). Suggests confounds or uncontrolled variables. Investigate causation before proceeding. (7) COMMUNICATE FINDINGS: Report to cycle 61: (a) Inventory of archive (what exists, metadata quality, comparability). (b) Sufficiency assessment (which hypotheses testable, which untested). (c) Recommendation: proceed with analysis, supplement with experiments, or redesign. (c) Declared gaps (what questions remain unanswered). (8) PLAN CYCLE 60 DEEP ANALYSIS (if sufficiency is HIGH): (a) Parse all 14 sessions for epistemic trajectories. (b) Construct comparison tables (baseline vs. variants vs. optimization). (c) Test hypotheses statistically (e.g., do meta/domain ratios differ significantly across conditions?). (d) Relate results to cycle 58 questions: Is meta-recursion essential, artifact, context-dependent, or design-choice? (e) Map findings to cycle 55 optimization questions: Should per-batch, gating, stable design be implemented? (f) Produce final hypothesis posterior: P(H1), P(H2), P(H3), P(H4) given archive data. VALIDATION DISCIPLINE (per cycle 58): Before committing to analysis or design changes: (a) State prior assumptions explicitly (what do we expect to find?). (b) Search actively for disconfirmation (data that contradicts each hypothesis). (c) Separate signal from noise (patterns robust across conditions vs. one-off anomalies). (d) Quantify uncertainty (confidence bands on estimates; acknowledge control limitations). (e) Document all decisions (which runs included, what metrics, what thresholds). This enables peer review and bias detection. TIMING: Cycle 60 should complete metadata extraction and gap assessment by turn 195. Communicate findings by turn 200. Deep analysis and hypothesis testing to follow in cycle 61 if archive sufficiency is HIGH. CRITICAL NOTE (for cycle 60-61): The archive is a gift: pre-existing data we did not have to generate. But it is also an obligation: we must treat it with epistemic care. Retrospective analysis is efficient but risky (confirmation bias, p-hacking, spurious correlation). Prospective experiment is slower but cleaner (controlled conditions, clear causal story). Cycle 59-60's job is to honestly assess which path is warranted. If archive is good, use it. If archive is poor, abandon it and run clean experiments. Do not force archive data to answer questions it cannot answer. Do not hide gaps. Declare losses openly. Let the data speak, and if it doesn't speak clearly, say so."
